insight_reviewer_julia_peters_role: Specialist in Gynecology & Obstetrics, Chief Medical Officer, SYNA · Focus on Perimenopause
insight_story_mrs_slide_1_subtitle: Based on your MRS-II questionnaire (score 38 out of 44)
insight_story_mrs_slide_1_body: This corresponds to severe perimenopausal symptoms. Right now, physical symptoms (hot flashes, sleep problems, heart palpitations, joint pain) are bothering you most. The MRS-II is an established questionnaire also used by doctors — your results are a valuable starting point.
insight_story_mrs_slide_2_headline: Physical symptoms are in the foreground
insight_story_mrs_slide_2_body: Your highest scores are in the physical domain. Hot flashes, sleep disruption, and joint discomfort often cluster in perimenopause — tracking them daily helps you and your doctor spot patterns.
insight_story_mrs_slide_3_body: Bring your MRS-II result to your next appointment. SYNA keeps your symptom history organized so conversations with your doctor are clearer and more focused.
insight_article_mrs_section_1_body: The Menopause Rating Scale II (MRS-II) is a validated 11-item questionnaire covering physical, psychological, and urogenital symptoms. Scores from 0 to 44 help classify symptom burden from none to severe.
insight_article_mrs_section_2_body: A score in the upper range suggests symptoms are significantly affecting daily life. This is common in perimenopause and does not mean you have to manage it alone.
insight_article_mrs_section_2_bullet_1: Physical symptoms (hot flashes, sleep, joints) often respond to lifestyle changes and medical options.
insight_article_mrs_section_2_bullet_2: Psychological symptoms (mood, irritability) may overlap with sleep disruption — treating one can help the other.
insight_article_mrs_section_2_bullet_3: Regular tracking makes it easier to see what helps over weeks, not just days.
insight_article_mrs_section_3_body: SYNA is not a substitute for medical advice. Use your score as a structured starting point for conversations with a healthcare provider who knows your full history.
insight_story_sleep_slide_1_subtitle: Based on your recent check-ins and wearable data
insight_story_sleep_slide_1_headline: Sleep is often the first thing to shift
insight_story_sleep_slide_1_body: In perimenopause, falling asleep, staying asleep, or waking too early becomes more common. Hormonal changes and night sweats both play a role — and poor sleep can amplify mood and hot flashes.
insight_story_sleep_slide_2_body: Progesterone and estrogen influence temperature regulation and sleep architecture. Even without night sweats, lighter sleep and earlier waking can appear months before cycle changes.
insight_story_sleep_slide_3_body: Cooler bedroom, consistent wake times, and limiting alcohol before bed are evidence-based first steps. SYNA helps you see whether these shifts correlate with better nights.
insight_article_sleep_section_1_body: Declining progesterone can reduce sleep depth, while estrogen fluctuations affect thermoregulation. The result: more awakenings, less restorative slow-wave sleep, and daytime fatigue.
insight_article_sleep_section_2_body: Sleep hygiene alone is not always enough in perimenopause, but it remains a useful foundation alongside medical options when needed.
insight_article_sleep_section_2_bullet_1: Keep the bedroom cool (around 18°C / 65°F) and use breathable bedding.
insight_article_sleep_section_2_bullet_2: Maintain a consistent wake time — even after a poor night.
insight_article_sleep_section_2_bullet_3: Limit caffeine after midday and alcohol within 3 hours of bedtime.
insight_article_sleep_section_3_body: If sleep problems persist for more than a few weeks or affect daily function, discuss them with your doctor. Treating sleep often improves mood and vasomotor symptoms too.
insight_story_heat_slide_1_subtitle: Based on your recent symptom check-ins
insight_story_heat_slide_1_body: Heat waves and sudden sweating are among the most common perimenopause symptoms. They can be brief or linger — tracking triggers helps you prepare and respond.
insight_story_heat_slide_2_body: Stress, alcohol, spicy food, tight clothing, and warm rooms are frequent triggers. Your SYNA check-ins help identify which patterns match your experience.
insight_story_heat_slide_3_body: Layered clothing, a fan at your desk, and cold water at hand are simple tools many women use daily. Medical options exist when lifestyle steps are not enough.
insight_article_heat_section_1_body: Estrogen fluctuations affect the hypothalamus — your internal thermostat. The brain may misread small temperature changes as overheating, triggering sweating and flushing.
insight_article_heat_section_2_body: Combining environmental adjustments with trigger awareness often reduces frequency and intensity over time.
insight_article_heat_section_2_bullet_1: Dress in layers you can remove quickly.
insight_article_heat_section_2_bullet_2: Keep a cool drink and fan accessible at work and at home.
insight_article_heat_section_2_bullet_3: Note episodes in SYNA to discuss patterns with your doctor.
insight_article_heat_section_3_body: Persistent or severe hot flashes deserve medical attention. Hormonal and non-hormonal treatments are available — your symptom log helps guide the conversation.
